# SWE-EVO: Benchmarking Coding Agents in Long-Horizon Software Evolution Scenarios

## Core Thesis

SWE-EVO argues that coding-agent performance looks materially weaker when the task shifts from local bug repair to real software evolution across large repositories, long specifications, and multi-file changes. Under this harder setup, even strong frontier models fall far below their scores on SWE-bench Verified, and the main failure pattern in unresolved runs appears to be not syntax collapse or tool misuse but failure to carry the specification through a long-horizon change safely.

## Why It Matters for Sapho

This matters because it cuts against a convenient reading of coding-agent progress. High benchmark performance on narrower repair tasks does not transfer cleanly to repository-scale evolution work, which is closer to the operational problem institutions actually face. For Sapho, the paper strengthens a doctrine of fail-closed evaluation: partial repair is not enough if regressions are introduced, and benchmark design must preserve exact versioning, regression sensitivity, and task scale rather than smoothing them away. It also warns against treating “more context” as a sufficient answer when the core weakness may be long-horizon instruction execution itself.

## Key Findings

- SWE-EVO is built around 48 software-evolution tasks drawn from release notes of seven mature open-source Python projects, not short isolated fixes.
- The benchmark is large-scale by construction: tasks touch an average of 21 files, are checked against test suites averaging 874 tests, and use problem statements averaging 2,390.5 words, with a maximum of 22,344 words.
- Repository scope is substantial: the mean non-test codebase is 363 files and 78K lines, with maxima of 1,046 files and 272K lines.
- The gold patches are also large: on average they edit 610.5 lines across 20.9 files and 51.0 functions, reinforcing that the benchmark targets software evolution rather than narrow patching.
- Admission and scoring are strict. Instances are kept only when the base commit exactly matches a repository version tag and the remaining patch still produces at least one FAIL_TO_PASS transition.
- Full success is regression-sensitive: an instance counts as resolved only when all FAIL_TO_PASS and PASS_TO_PASS tests pass, while Fix Rate drops to 0 if any previously passing test is broken.
- GPT-5 performs much worse here than on SWE-bench Verified: with PR or issue context it resolves 18.75% of instances with OpenHands and 20.83% with SWE-agent, versus 65.00% on SWE-bench Verified.
- Extra context helps only modestly. In the release-note-only setting, GPT-5 still reaches 14.58% with OpenHands and 16.67% with SWE-agent, leaving a large gap even after PR or issue material is added.
- In unresolved GPT-5 runs, more than 60% of failures are attributed to instruction-following mistakes, while syntax and tool-use failures are rare, though this result is bounded by a small Python-only benchmark and automated trajectory judging.

## Evidence and Findings

- The benchmark’s task geometry is materially larger than common repair settings: 48 tasks from seven mature Python repositories, average task scope of 21 files, average problem length of 2,390.5 words, and average codebase size of 363 files and 78K lines. This supports the conclusion that the benchmark is actually probing long-horizon repository evolution rather than local patch completion, which matters because claims about coding-agent readiness often rest on easier task families.
- Gold solutions are themselves large and distributed, averaging 610.5 edited lines across 20.9 files and 51.0 functions. That supports the reading that success requires sustained specification tracking and coherent multi-file coordination, not just finding a single faulty line or satisfying one narrow failing test.
- The benchmark is intentionally fail-closed at admission and scoring time: base commits must exactly match repository version tags, kept tasks must retain at least one FAIL_TO_PASS transition after patch isolation, and full resolution requires all FAIL_TO_PASS and PASS_TO_PASS tests to pass. This supports the conclusion that SWE-EVO is designed to preserve real regression risk, which matters because weaker scoring schemes can over-credit partial repair and hide breakage.
- The paper explicitly separates partial progress from end-to-end success. Fix Rate can distinguish models that tie on Resolved Rate—for example, under OpenHands, gpt-4.1 and gpt-oss-120b both resolve 2.08% of instances, but their Fix Rates are 4.65% and 2.08% respectively. That matters because it shows some systems repair more failing behavior than topline solved counts reveal, while still failing the stricter no-regression standard.
- GPT-5’s benchmark transfer is poor. With PR or issue context, it resolves 18.75% of SWE-EVO instances with OpenHands and 20.83% with SWE-agent, compared with 65.00% on SWE-bench Verified. This supports the conclusion that performance on established repair benchmarks overstates capability for long-horizon software evolution.
- Added context helps, but not enough to close the gap: release-note-only performance is 14.58% with OpenHands and 16.67% with SWE-agent, so the improvement from adding PR or issue material is real but modest. This matters because it weakens the story that the main missing ingredient is just more surrounding text; the bottleneck appears deeper than retrieval alone.

## Contradictions and Tensions

- The clearest tension is between partial repair and full success. A model can fix some FAIL_TO_PASS behavior yet still be scored as unresolved or receive a Fix Rate of 0 if it breaks any PASS_TO_PASS test. That means there is real intermediate competence in some trajectories, but the benchmark insists that regressions remain disqualifying.
- Additional PR or issue context does improve GPT-5 performance, so the benchmark does not show that context is irrelevant. But the gains are small relative to the overall gap with SWE-bench Verified, which creates a tension between “more context helps” and “more context does not solve the long-horizon problem.”
- The failure analysis points to instruction-following as the dominant issue in unresolved GPT-5 runs, but that diagnosis comes from automated judging of trajectories rather than direct test outcomes. The pattern is informative, yet less hard than the benchmark’s execution-based scoring.
- The benchmark is meant to challenge software-evolution ability, but it currently does so only in Python repositories built from release-note specifications. That creates a standing tension between a strong result about one realistic slice of coding-agent work and any broader claim about software engineering in general.

## Mechanism or Bounds

The strongest supported mechanism is operational rather than fully causal: as task scope expands across long specifications, large repositories, and multi-file edits, the agent must preserve intent over many coordinated changes while avoiding regressions under strict version-aligned evaluation. In that setting, failures appear to arise less from inability to emit valid code or invoke tools and more from losing the specification during the evolution process. The evidence supports this as a bounded explanation, not a universal one: the benchmark covers only 48 instances, only Python projects, and relies on release notes as the task specification, so the observed failure mix may partly reflect this specific framing rather than coding-agent behavior across all domains.

## Limits

The paper does not fully establish why long-horizon performance collapses; it shows the gap clearly but only partially explains it.
The failure taxonomy for unresolved runs relies on automated judgment rather than the same kind of direct test-grounded evidence used for resolution scoring.
The benchmark is currently small, with 48 instances, and restricted to Python projects, limiting confidence in cross-language generalization.
Tasks are derived from release notes, which are realistic but not exhaustive representations of how engineering work is specified in practice.

Because the benchmark is deliberately regression-sensitive, it is better read as a test of safe end-to-end software evolution than of raw patch-generation ability alone; that is a strength for deployment realism, but it also means headline scores should not be compared naively with looser benchmarks.
